rationale: Dhar et al. (2007) is cited in the theory development section, not just in a background list, to justify the key assumption that an implemental mind-set can increase additional unrelated purchases. The citing paper explicitly builds new hypotheses on top of that premise by adding regulatory focus compatibility from message framing and expiration restrictions as drivers/moderators of spillover. It also returns to Dhar et al. in the discussion to interpret observed basket effects as shopping momentum, indicating an ongoing conceptual role.
relation_description: The 2010 paper uses Dhar et al. (2007) as the basis for the shopping momentum idea that choices can spill over to unrelated purchases, then extends it by proposing that regulatory-fit cues in promotions and brand familiarity amplify or channel that momentum across the basket. It tests this extension in lab and field settings for basket size and composition outcomes.
